steps.title: Identify the sentence boundary problem
steps.content: After “in some versions,” the text continues with “the word was playfully rendered as 'bro!'” — this is an independent clause. Right now, it's joined to the relative clause “which is an Old English [blank] as 'hark!' or 'listen!' in some versions” with only a comma. When an independent clause follows material that is not yet complete as a sentence, joining them with a comma creates a comma splice.

steps.title: Apply the correct punctuation rule
steps.content: A period after “exclamation” creates a new sentence, properly separating the definition from what follows. The new sentence then reads: “Translated as 'hark!' or 'listen!' in some versions, the word was playfully rendered as 'bro!'” — here, “Translated as 'hark!' or 'listen!' in some versions” is a participial phrase that correctly introduces the main clause “the word was playfully rendered as 'bro!'” Choices A and C leave the comma splice in place (a comma still joins “in some versions” to the independent clause). Choice B creates a grammatical error — “is an exclamation and translated” cannot stand without a second verb form. That matches choice D — D is correct.
